summary: DHH argues against the industry convention of keeping pull requests small and short-lived. At Basecamp, pull requests run as long as needed to encompass a complete feature—sometimes up to six weeks under their Shape Up methodology. He acknowledges the trade-off of merge conflicts but contends the value of reviewing complete features in context far outweighs the cost. The core argument is that chopping features into small PRs degrades design standards and architectural cohesion by preventing reviewers from seeing the big picture.
key_points: Large PRs let reviewers see all trade-offs, design decisions, and changes in context together, preserving architectural cohesion
Merge conflicts from long-running branches can be mitigated by frequently merging main into the feature branch
The industry debate over PR size focuses too much on costs and not enough on the value of holistic code review
Small, disjointed PRs create negative externalities: missed simplifications, deferred cleanup that never happens, and code that's too shipped to change
Code review is just as powerful as a self-review tool—programmers benefit from seeing the complete diff of their own substantial work
money_quotes: My favorite part of doing code reviews is to see all the trade-offs, design decisions, and changes in context together.
That's what frustrates me a little about the debate over how long to let your pull requests run. It focuses mostly on the cost, not on the value.
High design standards and a cohesive architecture bank the rivers of clear code.
Code that didn't embrace a key insight to simplify the whole thing when viewed as a whole from 30,000 feet.
Code that doesn't leave the campsite in a better condition than it found it, because doing the cleanup is something for another pull request, on another day (by no one ever).
We often think of the code review as something done by others, but it's frequently just as powerful a method for a programmer to do to themselves.
Please don't chop up your pull requests until the cost of doing so definitively outpaces the value.
key_insight: Splitting features into small pull requests optimizes for reducing merge conflicts at the expense of the far more valuable ability to review a complete feature's design and architecture as a cohesive whole.
